title: Jira hierarchy, plans and scoping
topic: Atlassian Jira portfolio hierarchy, scoping containers (Project / Team / Plan), and access boundaries
summary: Jira uses a 3-level default hierarchy (Subtask/Standard/Epic) extensible to 6+ custom levels above Epic in Premium/Enterprise via Plans; Projects are hard permission and work-item boundaries; Plans are cross-project sandbox workspaces, not permission boundaries.
content:
Contents
  1. Summary
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Data Tables
  5. Sources
  6. Gaps and Limitations

Summary

Jira Software organises work in a three-tier default hierarchy (Subtask → Standard issue → Epic). Premium and Enterprise editions extend this upward with unlimited custom levels (Initiatives, Programs, Themes, etc.) configured through Plans (formerly Advanced Roadmaps). A Project is simultaneously a permission boundary, work-item container, and configuration container; every issue belongs to exactly one Project. Plans are cross-project planning workspaces — sandboxes for scheduling, capacity modelling, and dependency mapping — not permission containers. Issue Security Schemes provide row-level visibility control within a Project's boundaries.

Key Findings

  • Default hierarchy: 3 levels — Subtask (level -1), Standard issues (level 0), Epic (level 1). Initiatives and above require Premium/Enterprise.
  • Custom levels: unlimited in theory; community examples show 6+ (Themes → Goals/OKRs → Initiatives → Programs → Capabilities → Epic). Each new level is created at the top of the hierarchy stack.
  • Parent-child is stored as a single parent_id column on the Issue entity (introduced Oct 2023 in the Data Lake); the older epic-link and parent-link custom fields have been unified into the single parent field. Single parent only — no multi-parent.
  • Every issue belongs to exactly one Project. Subtasks must share their parent's Project. Standard-level issues (Stories, Bugs, Tasks) in company-managed projects can belong to an Epic in a different Project.
  • Rollups (story points, progress, status) are computed on-read in Plans from child issue data in Jira; no separate stored rollup values. Progress bar reflects percentage of child story points in Done.
  • Project Categories are flat labels (one per project), not a true hierarchy. They enable filtering and reporting but create no parent-project relationships.
  • Issue Security Schemes are row-level: a security level on an issue hides it from users not in that level, even within the same Project. Sub-tasks inherit their parent's security level automatically.
  • Team-managed projects have three simple access levels (open/limited/private) managed by the project admin. Company-managed projects use full Permission Schemes with 35+ granular permissions assignable to users, groups, and project roles.
  • Plans (Premium/Enterprise) can span multiple Projects. They pull work-item sources from boards, project spaces, or JQL filters. Plans are sandbox environments — changes are not written back to Jira until explicitly saved.
  • Teams in Plans are either site-wide Atlassian Teams or Plan-Only teams (ephemeral). Plan-Only team changes cannot be persisted until converted to Atlassian Teams.

Detailed Analysis

Q1 — Issue Type Hierarchy: Default Chain

Jira's documented default hierarchy uses numeric levels:

  • Level 1 (Epic) — large bodies of work; renamed but fixed position in free/standard tiers
  • Level 0 (Standard) — Story, Bug, Task; daily work units assignable to sprints and boards
  • Level -1 (Subtask) — granular breakdown of any standard issue; can only be a child, never a parent

Default work types per project type: Software spaces get Epic, Bug, Story, Task, Subtask; Business spaces get Task + Subtask; Service Management spaces have a different set (Change, Incident, Problem, etc.). The type is a field on the issue entity, not a separate table — Atlassian's public documentation does not expose the schema, but Analytics queries confirm a single Issue table with a parent issue ID column.

Q2 — Custom Hierarchy Levels (Advanced Roadmaps / Plans)

Custom levels above Epic are exclusively available on Jira Cloud Premium and Enterprise. Setup is three-step: (1) create a custom work type in Settings → Work types; (2) add it to a work type scheme; (3) assign it to a new hierarchy level in the Work type hierarchy menu (Settings → Products → Plans → Hierarchy configuration). New levels are always created at the top. No documented maximum — community examples demonstrate 6 levels above the default three. Changes are site-wide and apply to all company-managed projects using that work type scheme. The documentation warns that changes cannot be undone and will break existing parent-child relationships between work items if levels are removed.

Q3 — Parent-Child Mechanics

As of late 2023/2024, Jira consolidated the historic Epic Link and Parent Link custom fields into a single unified parent field. At the storage level, the Atlassian Data Lake exposes this as a Parent issue ID column on the Issue entity — effectively a single-parent foreign key. Multi-parent is not supported. Typed links (e.g. "blocks", "is cloned by") exist separately in the issuelink table for non-hierarchical relationships. Cycle prevention: the hierarchy enforces strict directionality — sub-tasks cannot be parents, and skip-level parenting is not permitted (e.g. an Epic cannot be a direct parent of a Subtask skipping Standard level). The API deprecates epic-link and parentEpic JQL functions in favour of parent().

Q4 — Strategy-Execution Boundary

Jira's own documentation and third-party analysis draw the strategy/execution boundary at the Initiative level: Initiatives (and levels above) represent strategic themes managed by leadership on quarter-to-year timeframes; Epics and below are execution-layer items managed by delivery teams on weeks-to-months cycles. This boundary is not enforced by different storage or access models — both Initiatives and Epics are issue records of different types in the same Issue table. The distinction is workflow, field configuration, and who has permission to create/edit items at each level. Jira Align (separate product) is Atlassian's answer for enterprise portfolio strategy above the Jira hierarchy.

Q5 — Rollups

Rollups are computed on-read in Plans, not stored as separate column values. Progress of a parent issue is calculated from child issue completion: if an Epic has 5 Stories each worth 1 story point and 3 are Done, the Epic shows 60% progress. The calculation uses the Done workflow status category. For unestimated issues, progress shows 0% or 100% (binary). Story point rollups from sub-tasks to parents round to one decimal place. Users click "Calculate" to refresh in the older Portfolio data model; in Plans the timeline reflects live data. Rollup fields visible in Plans cannot be edited — they are derived views of source Jira data. Configurable aspects: which hierarchy levels are displayed, whether completed items are included, and custom date-range windows.

Q6 — Reparenting

A Story can be reparented to a different Epic through the Parent field in the issue view. The reparenting event is recorded in the issue changelog (historically via IssueParentAssociation changelog type, replacing deprecated Epic Link changelog entries). Subtasks follow their parent issue's project — they cannot be reparented to an Epic in a different project. If a parent issue type is changed (e.g. Epic converted to Story), child issues lose their parent link and become orphans in the backlog. Cross-project constraints: standard-level issues can point to an Epic in a different company-managed project; subtasks cannot cross project boundaries.

Q7 — Orphan Handling

Jira is permissive with orphans — there is no system-enforced rule that an Epic must have an Initiative parent, or that a Story must have an Epic. Issues without parents are silently valid and appear in the backlog ungrouped. In Plans, orphaned issues (those without a parent at their expected level) appear in the timeline view as unparented items and cannot contribute to parent-level rollups. The Plans warning system can surface issues with missing parents under the "Warnings" filter, but this is a UI flag, not an enforcement block. Community discussions confirm that adding a new hierarchy level mid-project causes all existing Epics to become orphaned (no Initiative parent) because skip-level parenting is not retroactively created.

Q8 — Subtree Filtering UX

Plans provides hierarchy-range filtering: users set a "From level" and "To level" to scope the visible hierarchy band. The "Show full hierarchy" toggle, when enabled alongside a filter, ensures all child issues of matching parents are displayed even if they don't independently match filter criteria. JQL supports portfolioChildIssuesOf() to retrieve all descendants below a given issue regardless of depth. The filter panel in Plans includes: hierarchy level range, assignee, team, sprint, release, label, work type, status, and dependency filters. Scope can also be set at the Plan source level (boards, spaces, or JQL filters) which pre-limits which issues are loaded into the plan at all.

Q9 — Project Definition

A Jira Project is simultaneously three things: (1) a permission boundary — permission schemes and issue security schemes are applied at project level; (2) a work-item container — every issue has a mandatory project field and belongs to exactly one project; (3) a configuration container — workflows, screens, field configurations, components, and versions are configured per project (company-managed) or self-contained (team-managed). Projects have a unique key (e.g. "PROJ") used in issue identifiers (PROJ-123). There is no nesting of projects natively.

Q10 — Project Nesting

Project Categories are flat labels — one category per project, managed by Jira admins. They enable advanced-search filtering and reporting grouped by category but create no parent-child hierarchy between projects. Feature requests for nested project categories exist in the Atlassian community (JRACLOUD-5721, JRASERVER-4578) with no planned implementation. The only multi-project grouping with hierarchical semantics is Plans, which spans projects as a planning layer.

Q11 — What Lives Inside a Project

Company-managed project contains: Issues (the core work items), Boards (Scrum/Kanban), Workflows (status transition maps), Screens and Field Configurations (which fields appear on create/view/edit), Components (feature groupings within the project), Versions/Releases, Permission Scheme (project-scoped access), Issue Security Scheme (row-level visibility), Notification Scheme, and Issue Type Scheme. An issue belongs to exactly one project — this is a hard constraint. The project field on an issue cannot be null. Moving an issue to another project is possible via the "Move Issues" permission and changes the issue key prefix.

Q12 — Team Entity

Teams in Jira exist in two forms: Atlassian Teams (site-wide or org-wide, created in the Atlassian Teams directory, usable across all plans) and Plan-Only Teams (scoped to a single plan, ephemeral — changes cannot be saved until converted to Atlassian Teams). Teams are not a permission boundary; they are an organisational grouping for capacity planning and work assignment visualisation. In Plans, a team receives work item assignments, has capacity measured in story points or hours per sprint, and serves as a filter axis on the timeline. Atlassian Teams can include members and appear in the user directory; Plan-Only teams are placeholder groupings with no persistent member records.

Q13 — Plans: Saved View, Permission Boundary, or Planning Workspace?

Plans are planning workspaces, not permission boundaries or mere saved views. Key characteristics: (a) Plans pull from up to three source types — boards, project spaces, and JQL filters — allowing cross-project scope; (b) changes made in Plans (date adjustments, team assignments, parent-child links at the Initiative level) are held in a sandbox and only written back to Jira issues when explicitly saved by the user; (c) Plans do not restrict who can see issues — access to a Plan does not override project-level permissions; users see only issues they already have Browse Project permission for; (d) Plans support scenario planning (what-if analysis) without affecting live Jira data; (e) Plans are Premium/Enterprise only. A Plan is not a saved filter — it has its own configuration state, team definitions, hierarchy levels, and dependency map.

Q14 — Cross-Project Work

Cross-project Epic/Story relationships: In company-managed projects, a Story in Project B can have its parent Epic in Project A. This is the primary native cross-project hierarchy mechanism. Team-managed projects are fully isolated — no cross-project parent-child relationships are supported. Subtasks must always be in the same project as their parent. Plans enable cross-project visibility for all levels: an Initiative can aggregate Epics from multiple projects; the Plan timeline shows dependency arrows across project boundaries. Cross-space releases in Plans align milestone dates across multiple project spaces.

Q15 — Permissions

Jira has three permission layers: (1) Global permissions (site-wide — system admin, user admin, bulk change); (2) Project permission schemes (35+ named permissions per project, applied to users/groups/project roles); (3) Issue Security Schemes (row-level, per-issue visibility). The primary project permissions relevant to access include: Browse Projects (view the project and its issues), Administer Projects (manage roles, components, versions), Create/Edit/Delete/Move Issues, Transition Issues, Set Issues Security, Assign Issues, Link Issues, Manage Sprints, and View Development Tools. Permissions are granted to: individual users, Jira groups, project roles (Administrators, Developers, Users — role membership is project-scoped), issue roles (Current Assignee, Reporter, Project Lead), or "Anyone" (anonymous). Not available on Free tier.

Q16 — Issue Security

Issue Security Schemes provide row-level security for individual issues within a project. A scheme defines one or more security levels; each level specifies which users, groups, or roles can see issues at that level. If an issue has a security level set, only users matching that level's criteria can view it — even project members with Browse Projects permission are excluded if they don't match. Sub-tasks automatically inherit their parent issue's security level. The "Set Issue Security" project permission controls who can assign security levels to issues. Default security levels ensure newly created issues are consistently protected. When an issue moves between projects (via Move Issues), it adopts the default security level of the destination project's scheme, or becomes visible to all project members if no scheme exists.

Table 3: Permission Layers

LayerScopeMechanismGranularity
Global permissionsEntire Jira siteGroups assigned to global rolesCoarse (system admin, user admin, bulk ops)
Project Permission SchemeProject boundaryNamed permissions assigned to users/groups/roles35+ named operations (Browse, Create, Edit, Move, etc.)
Issue Security SchemeIndividual issue (row-level)Security levels with user/group/role membershipPer-issue; sub-tasks inherit from parent

Table 4: Plans — Key Properties

PropertyValue
EditionPremium and Enterprise only
Work item sourcesBoards (scrum/kanban), Project spaces, JQL filters
Cross-projectYes — a Plan can span unlimited projects
Permission boundaryNo — project permissions still govern visibility within a Plan
Sandbox modelYes — changes held until user explicitly saves to Jira
RollupsOn-read from child issue data; not stored separately
Team typesAtlassian Teams (site-wide) or Plan-Only (ephemeral)
Hierarchy levelsReflects site hierarchy config; custom levels visible in Plan timeline

Table 5: Parent-Child Relationship Mechanics

AspectBehaviour
StorageSingle parent_id column on Issue entity; unified from legacy epic-link + parent-link fields
Multi-parentNot supported — single parent FK only
Typed linksSeparate issuelink table for non-hierarchical relationships (blocks, clones, relates to)
Cycle preventionSubtasks cannot be parents; skip-level parenting not permitted
ReparentingAllowed via Parent field; recorded in issue changelog; subtasks cannot cross project boundary
Orphan on type changeConverting Epic to Story removes child issues parent links; children returned to backlog as orphans
Max children displayed500 child items in parent UI; beyond that, search required

Gaps and Limitations

  • Internal schema not public: Atlassian does not publish the Jira Cloud database schema. Parent-child storage confirmed via Analytics documentation as a parent_id-style column; exact table name and constraints are inferred, not directly documented.
  • Cycle prevention mechanics: No official documentation explicitly describes cycle-detection algorithms; behaviour inferred from documented constraints (subtasks cannot be parents; skip-level not permitted).
  • Maximum custom hierarchy levels: No hard maximum documented; practical guidance suggests 6 levels as an organisational limit.
  • Jira Align distinction: Atlassian's separate Jira Align product covers the Portfolio/Program layer above Jira's hierarchy (Themes, Epics at Align level, Features, Stories mapped from Jira). This research covers Jira Software/Plans only, not Jira Align.
  • Cloud vs Data Center divergence: Some permission and hierarchy behaviours differ between Jira Cloud and Data Center editions; this report focuses on Jira Cloud.
  • Plans permission model detail: While confirmed that Plans does not create a permission boundary, detailed behaviour of restricted issues appearing in cross-project Plans views was not fully documented in available sources.
